register: Private aviation operators — plain English, no insider jargon. NEVER use: 'Part 135', 'ARGUS', 'Wyvern', 'fractional ownership', 'jet card', 'empty-leg', 'flight department KPI'. Replace with: 'private jet charter company', 'people who fly private', 'private flight shopper', 'inquiry form', 'quote request'. Tone: discreet, surgical, neutral. No hype, no emoji, no high-pressure. Jeremy Miner NEPQ + PAS structure.

A.name: Pattern Interrupt + Soft Disarm
A.desc: NEPQ + PAS. Best for never-engaged private jet charter operators with verified phone. Permission-based open, surgical problem reveal, two leaks + withheld third. ~205s.

beats.title: Disarm + Permission
beats.line: I know this is out of the blue — feel free to hang up if I caught you wrong. I'm not even sure I'm reaching the right person — you're with {company}, correct? I pulled up your website this morning. We do a quiet review every quarter on the websites of private aviation operators — looking at the page somebody actually sees after they click an ad or land from a Google search.
beats.followup: Totally fair — I'll be quick. Do you have 90 seconds, or did I catch you mid-something?
beats.note: Miner low-status open. 'Not even sure I'm reaching the right person' lowers defenses. Pause after 'every quarter.' Don't fill silence. HNW operators reward composure.

beats.title: Surface the Buyer's Reality
beats.line: Quick context on what we actually look at — when somebody who flies private lands on your website to request a quote, there's a small window — maybe 6 to 8 seconds — where their brain decides 'these people are serious' or 'these people aren't quite there yet.' Most operators we review lose them in that window. Not because of price. Not because of the aircraft. Because of three structural things on the page that have nothing to do with the airplanes themselves.
beats.followup: Most operators say that — and they're usually right that the page looks fine. The leak is rarely visual. It's structural.
beats.note: PAS — Problem. Plain English replacement for the 'flight-shopper' jargon. The 8-second window is universally true in luxury commerce. Don't sell hard yet.

beats.title: Reveal Two of Three Leaks
beats.line: On {company} specifically, two things jumped out. First — {leak_1}. Second — {leak_2}. Neither one is fatal on its own. But on a charter that books at $25,000 to $80,000 a flight, even a small drop in the inquiry-to-booking rate is real money walking off the page every week. There's a third leak that's actually the most costly of the three — I'd rather walk you through that one on a 15-minute screen share than burn your time on the phone.
beats.followup: I promise the third one's worth the 15 minutes. It's the one almost every operator misses, and it's the easiest of the three to fix.
beats.note: DO NOT reveal leak 3 on the phone. The third leak IS the meeting. If they push: 'I respect that — I'd rather earn the 15 minutes than spoil the value of the review. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Soft Diagnostic Question
beats.line: Out of curiosity — and you don't have to answer this — do you know roughly what percentage of the people who fill out your quote form actually end up on a flight? Most operators we ask have a feel for it, but they're not tracking it specifically. The leaks I just mentioned affect that number directly.
beats.followup: Yeah, most operators don't track it precisely — it's hard to attribute. But it's the number that moves when the leaks get fixed.
beats.note: Miner awareness question. If they don't know the number, you've surfaced a gap they can't unsee. If they DO know it, that's an even better signal — they care about the math.

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: We work only with private aviation operators — private jet charter companies, jet card programs, and shared-ownership operators. We don't touch leisure travel, we don't touch commercial. And we're discreet — nothing gets posted on LinkedIn, no case studies, no name-drops. The work stays between us and the principal. That matters to the kind of clients you fly.
responses: Who else have you worked with?
beats.followup: Happy to share references after the review call if it ends up being the right fit on both sides.
beats.note: Plain English only — NEVER say industry jargon (regulatory tier codes, safety-audit brand names, ownership-model terms). Use: 'private jet charter companies, jet card programs, shared-ownership operators.' NEVER name-drop specific operators — discretion IS the product.

beats.title: Book the Time
beats.line: Two ways to do this. Either I send the written review — about 8 pages with screenshots, normally a $4,500 deliverable, yours to keep — or we jump on a 15-minute screen share with whoever owns the website on your side, and {strategist} walks you through it live. Most operators prefer the screen share because it's easier to ask questions in real time. Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern?
beats.followup: Perfect — what's the best email for the calendar invite? Let me read it back to make sure I get it right.
beats.note: Some HNW operators take the document and come back 2-3 weeks later. Log them as 'doc-only follow-up' in notes — they convert at ~22% on the second touch. Always read email back verbatim — wrong email is the #1 no-show cause.

B.name: Curiosity Hook + Sandler Negative Reverse
B.desc: NEPQ Belief-Building + Sandler. Best for re-engagement, referrals, or operators who ghosted on a prior touch. Lowers the bar by telling them you're easy to say no to. Pure layman's English. ~180s.

beats.title: Curiosity Hook
beats.line: Hi — I work with private jet charter operators on something most of them don't realize is quietly costing them somewhere around 30% of the inquiries they're paying to generate. Got 90 seconds for me to tell you what it is? If it doesn't apply to {company}, I'll let you go.
beats.followup: Quick context — I'm not selling anything on this call. I'm telling you what I see on your website, and you decide if it's worth a follow-up.
beats.note: If they say 'what is it?' — you've already won the open. Slow down. Don't rush the reveal. Composure signals you're not a typical agency cold-caller.

beats.title: Build the Belief
beats.line: Without going into the weeds — when somebody with the means to fly private lands on a charter website, there's roughly an 8-second window where they decide 'these people are serious' or 'these people aren't quite there yet.' Most operators lose them around the 6-second mark. Not because of price. Not because of the airplanes. Because of three structural things on the page that have nothing to do with the fleet.
beats.followup: I'll show you specifically what's happening on your page in a minute. Quick question first — does anyone on your team track how many people who land on the page actually fill out the quote form?
beats.note: Miner: 'Does anyone on your team track' is softer than 'do you track' — invites a 'no, my marketing person does' which gives you the next call. If they say nobody tracks it, that's diagnostic gold.

beats.title: Two Leaks Revealed, Third Withheld
beats.line: I reviewed {company}'s website this morning. {leak_1} is one. {leak_2} is the second. The third I'd rather show you on a screen share — it's the one that costs the most of the three, and it's actually the easiest to fix. It's the one almost every operator misses.
beats.followup: I'll show you Thursday or Friday — I promise it's worth the 15 minutes. If it isn't, you've still got two free fixes from this call.
beats.note: Hold the third leak. The third leak IS the meeting. If they push to know: 'I respect that — I'd rather earn the 15 minutes than spoil what we put together. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Lower the Stakes (Sandler Negative Reverse)
beats.line: Look — it's not a sales call. We don't sell anything on the review call itself. {strategist} walks you through what we'd change, in what order, and then you either do it yourself, hand it to your in-house team, or — if it ends up being the right fit on both sides — we do it for you. Most operators take the document and run. That's fine.
beats.followup: No catch. {strategist} is running a handful personally this quarter — that's the only ask. And we don't publish names, so it's truly anonymous.
beats.note: Sandler Negative Reverse: telling them you're EASY to say no to lowers resistance. Counterintuitive — but it works on HNW. They hate being sold to. NEVER use 'case-study purposes' here — too transactional.

beats.title: Close on Time
beats.line: Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern — which works better? {strategist} blocks the time himself, so whichever you pick is locked.
beats.followup: Perfect — calendar invite incoming. Best email for that? Let me read it back to make sure I have it right.
beats.note: Don't offer 'whenever works' — give two specific slots. HNW prospects respect bounded calendars and distrust open ones. Read email back.

C.name: Paid-Search Leak + The Math
C.desc: PAS + Specificity Cluster + Loss-Frame. Best for operators visibly running paid search (verify on Google before dialing). Math-led — anchors on what they're paying per click. Plain English only. ~180s.

beats.title: Paid-Search Anchor
beats.line: I know this is out of the blue — quick reason for the call. I see {company} is running paid search on terms like '{primary_kw}'. Clicks in your category are running roughly ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} each right now. Got 60 seconds?
beats.followup: Quick — it's not about the ads themselves. The ads are doing their job. The leak is on what happens after the click — on the page itself.
beats.note: PRE-CALL: verify they actually run ads by Googling their primary keyword from {market}. If you don't see them on the SERP, switch to Variant A or B. This open burns credibility if they're not on paid search.

beats.title: The Numbers in Plain English
beats.line: Here's the math in plain English. Say your average click is around ${cpc_high}. If 1.5 out of every 100 clicks fills out the quote form — which is typical for the charter websites we review — you're paying for 100 clicks to get about 1 or 2 real inquiries. That's somewhere in the low thousands of ad-dollars per qualified quote. If we move that page from 1.5% up to 3% — well inside normal range for a properly built private aviation site — you cut your cost-per-quote roughly in half. Same ad spend, double the quotes. Or same quotes, half the spend.
beats.followup: If you're higher — is that higher than 3%, or higher than 1.5%? Big difference. Most operators we review run somewhere between 0.8% and 2.2%.
beats.note: Don't argue numbers. Anchor on the LIFT, not the current state. If they claim 5%+, ask: 'Out of curiosity — is that measured at quote-submit, or at booked-flight? Different metric.'

beats.title: Where the Spend Is Leaking
beats.line: I looked at where the ad spend is landing on your end. Two things jumped out. {leak_1}, and {leak_2}. Neither of those would be hard to fix — they're literally things a developer can change in an afternoon. There's a third leak that's actually the most expensive of the three — that one I'd rather walk you through on a 15-minute screen share with the page open in front of us.
beats.followup: If you're asking 'how much' — the review itself is free. The fix depends on what we find. Some operators do it in-house once they see the diagnosis.
beats.note: The phrase 'fix in an afternoon' is intentional — makes the win feel close. The third-leak withhold creates the same pull as Variants A and B.

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: Quick on us — we work only with private aviation operators. Private jet charter companies, jet card programs, shared-ownership operators. We don't touch leisure travel or commercial airlines. And we're discreet — names stay between us and the principal. Same discretion you'd want for your own clients.
beats.followup: Happy to share references after the review call if it's the right fit on both sides. The discretion you'd want for your clients is the same discretion we apply to ours.
beats.note: Plain English only — no industry jargon. Use: 'private jet charter companies, jet card programs, shared-ownership operators.' NEVER name-drop on a cold call.

beats.title: Close the Time
beats.line: Two options. Either I send you the full written review — about 8 pages with screenshots, normally a $4,500 deliverable, yours to keep — or 15 minutes Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern on a screen share with {strategist} and whoever owns the website on your side. Which works better?
beats.followup: Great — what's your best email? And anyone else from your side who should be on the call? Operations director or whoever owns the website usually.
beats.note: Read email back verbatim. Operators routinely give the wrong email and it kills the show-up rate. Always confirm second person.
